data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was an emergent property of successful earlier adaptation: Mycenaean and Hittite palaces had optimized for exactly the inter-regional equilibrium they destroyed, making short-term stabilizing responses (defensive mobilization, hoarding, alliance-seeking) systemically destructive via prisoner's dilemma dynamics", "mechanism": "Subgame-perfect irrationality: given mutual knowledge of finite copper/tin reserves and observed defection (first Hittite or Mycenaean palace to militarize against Sea Peoples triggers anticipatory militarization by neighbors), rational individual responses produce collectively catastrophic arms races and trade embargoes. Egypt's geographical remove makes it a bystander, not player, in this game.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt as strategic actor", "Climate as exogenous forcing"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Evidence of coordinated anti-Sea Peoples alliance among Mycenaean, Hittite, and Levantine polities during 1220-1180 BC", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Linear B or Hittite texts showing palace recognition of arms-race dynamics and deliberate restraint", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian diplomatic correspondence showing Egyptian mediation or alliance-brokering between collapsing palaces", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Game-theoretic inversion: collapse was not failure to adapt but successful maladaptation. Most atypical in denying Egypt explanatory centrality—it survived by not being in the game, not by superior institutional design. See Schelling on 'focal point' destruction."}
